name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
مَا جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِنۢ بَحِيرَةٍ وَلَا سَآئِبَةٍ وَلَا وَصِيلَةٍ وَلَا حَامٍ ۙ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ ۖ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (١٠٣)
```

```english
Allah has not prescribed any such thing as Bahirah, Sa’ibah, Waseelah, or Haam; but those who are faithless fabricate lies against Allah, and most of them do not exercise their reason. (103)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعَالَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ وَإِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ قَالُوا۟ حَسْبُنَا مَا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَنَآ ۚ أَوَلَوْ كَانَ ءَابَآؤُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ (١٠٤)
```

```english
When they are told, ‘Come to what Allah has sent down and [come] to the Apostle,’ they say, ‘Sufficient for us is what we have found our fathers following.’ What, even if their fathers did not know anything and were not guided?! (104)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما جَعَلَ اللهُ مِنْ بَحِيرَةٍ وَلا سائِبَةٍ وَلا وَصِيلَةٍ وَلا حامٍ** (Allah has not prescribed any bahirah, or sa’ibah, or wasilah, or ham) [5:103]. These are categories of livestock (الأنعام) for which the people of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) observed certain rulings based on veneration and a form of liberation (التحرير) — *the act of setting an animal free from labor or use for the sake of idols*. Allah, the Glorified, has denied having prescribed any of this. The denied "prescribing" (الجعل) pertains to their descriptions (أوصاف) rather than their essences (ذوات), for their essences are undoubtedly created by Allah, the Glorified. Likewise, their descriptions, insofar as they are merely descriptions, are His creation. Rather, what accepts attribution to Him, the Exalted, or denial thereof, are these descriptions insofar as they were the sources of rulings they claimed for them. Thus, the denial of prescribing the *bahirah* and its counterparts in the verse is a denial of the lawfulness (مشروعية) of the rulings attributed to them that were well-known among them.

Although there is disagreement regarding the meaning of the names of these four categories of livestock—which leads to disagreement in identifying their specific rulings, as you will see—it is certain that their rulings were based on a type of liberation and veneration by respecting their condition. Three of them—the *bahirah* (البحيرة), the *sa’ibah* (السائبة), and the *ham* (الحامي)—pertain to camels, while one—the *wasilah* (الوصيلة)—pertains to sheep.

As for the *bahirah* (البحيرة), it is mentioned in *al-Majma‘* that it was a she-camel that, if she gave birth to five litters and the last was a male, they would slit her ear (بحروا أذنها) — *that is, they made a wide incision in it* — and they would refrain from riding or slaughtering her. She would not be driven away from water nor barred from pasture, and if an exhausted traveler encountered her, he would not ride her; this is narrated from al-Zajjaj.

It is also said that if a she-camel gave birth to five litters, they would look at the fifth litter: if it was a male, they slaughtered it and both men and women ate it; if it was a female, they slit her ear, and she was the *bahirah*. Thereafter, her wool would not be shorn, the Name of Allah would not be mentioned over her if she were slaughtered, she would not be used for carrying loads, and women were forbidden from tasting any of her milk or benefiting from her. Her milk and benefits were exclusively for men until she died; upon her death, both men and women shared in eating her. This is narrated from Ibn Abbas. It is also said that the *bahirah* is the daughter of the *sa’ibah*, as narrated from Muhammad ibn Ishaq.

As for the *sa’ibah* (السائبة), it is mentioned in *al-Majma‘* that it refers to what they used to set free (يسيبونه). If a man vowed to return from a journey or recover from an illness or the like, he would say: "My she-camel is a *sa’ibah*." She would then be like the *bahirah* in that she was not utilized, nor kept from water, nor barred from pasture. This is from al-Zajjaj and is the view of ‘Alqamah.

It is also said that she is the one set free for the idols—meaning emancipated for them. A man would set free whatever he wished of his wealth and bring it to the custodians (السدنة) — *the servants of their gods* — who would then feed her milk to wayfarers and the like. This is from Ibn Abbas and Ibn Mas‘ud.

It is also said that the *sa’ibah* is a she-camel that, if she gave birth to ten consecutive females without a male among them, she was set free. They would not ride her, shear her wool, or drink her milk except for a guest. Whatever female she gave birth to after that would have its ear slit and then be left free with its mother; that offspring was the *bahirah*. This is from Muhammad ibn Ishaq.

As for the *wasilah* (الوصيلة), it is mentioned in *al-Majma‘* that it pertains to sheep. If a ewe gave birth to a female, it was for them, and if she gave birth to a male, they designated it for their gods. But if she gave birth to a male and a female together, they would say: "She has reached (وصلت) her brother," and they would not slaughter the male for their gods. This is from al-Zajjaj.

It is also said that if a ewe gave birth to seven litters, and the seventh was a male, they slaughtered it for their gods and its meat was for men only. If it was a female, they let it live and it remained among the sheep. If she gave birth in the seventh litter to both a male and a female, they said: "The sister has reached her brother due to his sanctity (حرمته) over us," so both became forbidden. Thus, the benefit and milk were for men and not women. This is from Ibn Mas‘ud and Muqatil.

It is also said that the *wasilah* is a ewe that, if she gave birth to ten females in five litters without a male among them, she was made a *wasilah*. They would say: "She has reached (وصلت)," and whatever she gave birth to after that was for males and not females. This is from Muhammad ibn Ishaq.

As for the *ham* (الحامي), it is mentioned in *al-Majma‘* that it is the male camel. When ten litters had been sired from the stallion's loins, the Arabs would say: "He has protected (حمى) his back," so he would not be used for carrying loads, nor barred from water or pasture. This is from Ibn Abbas and Ibn Mas‘ud, and it is the view of Abu ‘Ubaydah and al-Zajjaj.

It is also said that it is the stallion that, when its grandson is sired from it, they say: "He has protected his back," so he is not ridden. This is from al-Farra’.

Although they differ in the interpretation of these names, it is highly probable that this difference arises from the varying tastes (سلائق) of different peoples in their customs (سنن); such variations are common in the customs prevalent among primitive (همجية) peoples.

In any case, the verse is directed at denying the rulings they had fabricated for these four categories of livestock, attributing them to Allah, the Glorified. This is evidenced first by His saying: **ما جَعَلَ اللهُ** (Allah has not prescribed...), and second by His saying: **وَلكِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللهِ الْكَذِبَ** (but the faithless fabricate lies against Allah) [5:103].

Therefore, His saying: **وَلكِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا** (but the faithless...) serves as an answer to a presumed question. It is as if when it was said: **ما جَعَلَ اللهُ مِنْ بَحِيرَةٍ** (Allah has not prescribed any bahirah...), it was asked: "Then what is this that these disbelievers claim?" The answer was that it is a fabrication of lies against Allah on their part. Then, the explanation was furthered by the saying: **وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْقِلُونَ** (and most of them do not apply reason) [5:103]. Its meaning is that they differ in this fabrication: most of them fabricate what they fabricate while they do not apply reason (لا يَعْقِلُونَ), while a few of these fabricators do understand the truth and know that what they attribute to Him, the Exalted, is a fabrication. These are the followed leaders who are obeyed by others and who manage their affairs; they are the people of obstinacy (عناد) and persistence in falsehood (لجاج).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعالَوْا إِلى ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ** (And when it is said to them, ‘Come to what Allah has sent down...) [5:104] to the end of the verse. This recounts the invitation to what Allah sent down to the Messenger, whose sole duty is delivery (البلاغ). Thus, the invitation is an invitation to the Truth (الحق), which is veracity (الصدق) free from fabrication, and knowledge (العلم) purified from ignorance. For the previous verse combined fabrication and lack of reasoning on their side, leaving nothing for that which they are invited to—namely, the side of Allah, the Glorified—except veracity and knowledge.

However, they did not repel it except through blind imitation (التقليد), as they said: **حَسْبُنا ما وَجَدْنا عَلَيْهِ آباءَنا** (Sufficient for us is what we found our fathers following) [5:104]. Although imitation (التقليد) — *the following of another's authority without independent proof* — is correct in certain instances and under certain conditions—namely, the recourse of the ignorant to the learned, which is a principle established in the conduct of human society in all life's affairs where it is not feasible for an individual to obtain direct knowledge of the vital path he must tread—the imitation of an ignorant person in his ignorance, meaning the recourse of an ignorant person to another ignorant person like himself, is blameworthy in the way of the rational (سنة العقلاء). Just as it is blameworthy for a learned person to turn to another learned person by abandoning what he can independently act upon and taking what another knows.

Therefore, Allah, the Exalted, rejected it with His saying: **أَوَلَوْ كانَ آباؤُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً وَلا يَهْتَدُونَ** (What, even if their fathers knew nothing and were not guided?) [5:104]. Its meaning is that reason—if reason were present—does not permit a person to turn to one who possesses neither knowledge (علم) nor guidance (اهتداء). This is the law of life: it does not permit treading a path whose dangers are not secured, and whose description is unknown, neither through independence nor by following one who has expertise in it.

Perhaps the addition of His saying: **وَلا يَهْتَدُونَ** (and were not guided) to His saying: **لا يَعْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً** (knew nothing) is to complete the necessary conditions of the discourse according to the reality. For the recourse of the ignorant to his like, though blameworthy, is only so if the one followed is exactly like the follower in his ignorance, not distinguished from him by anything. However, if the followed one himself treads the path by the guidance of a learned expert, then he is guided (مهتدٍ) in his conduct, and there is no blame on the one who follows him in his journey and imitates him in treading the path. For the matter ultimately traces back to knowledge (العلم) in the end, like one who follows a person knowledgeable about the path, and then another ignorant person follows him.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: **أَوَلَوْ كانَ آباؤُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً** (What, even if their fathers knew nothing) would not have been sufficient to complete the proof (الحجة) against them, due to the possibility that their fathers whom they followed in imitation were guided through the imitation of guiding scholars. Thus, the ruling of blame would not apply to them, nor would the proof be completed against them. This was refuted by stating that their fathers knew nothing and were not guided, and there is no justification for following one in such a state.

Since it was established from the first verse—namely His saying: **ما جَعَلَ اللهُ مِنْ بَحِيرَةٍ** (Allah has not prescribed any bahirah...)—that they are divided between those who do not reason at all (and they are the majority) and those who are obstinate and arrogant, it follows that they are far removed from being worthy of direct address or the presentation of proof. Therefore, the proof in the second verse was not presented to them in the form of a direct address; rather, the speech was directed toward others, turning away from facing them directly, as it was said: **أَوَلَوْ كانَ آباؤُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً وَلا يَهْتَدُونَ** (What, even if their fathers knew nothing and were not guided?).

A scholarly and ethical discussion on the meaning of imitation (التقليد) has already been presented in the first volume of this work, which you may refer to.

It is clear from the verse that turning to the Book of Allah and to His Messenger—which is turning to the Sunnah (السنة) — *the established practice and teachings of the Prophet* — is not in any way part of blameworthy imitation.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from al-Saduq, with his chain of narration to Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **ما جَعَلَ اللهُ مِنْ بَحِيرَةٍ وَلا سائِبَةٍ وَلا وَصِيلَةٍ وَلا حامٍ** (Allah has not prescribed any bahirah, or sa’ibah, or wasilah, or ham). He said: "The people of the Age of Ignorance, if a she-camel gave birth to two offspring in one litter, they would say: 'She has reached (وصلت),' so they would not deem her slaughter or eating lawful. If she gave birth to ten, they made her a *sa’ibah*, and they would not deem her back [for riding] or her eating lawful. The *ham* was the stallion of the camels; they would not deem it lawful. So Allah sent down that He had not forbidden any of that."

He said: Then Ibn Babawayh said: "It has been narrated that the *bahirah* is the she-camel that, if she produced five litters—and if the fifth was a male—they slaughtered it and both men and women ate it. If the fifth was a female, they slit her ear—that is, they cut it—and her meat and milk were forbidden to women. When she died, she became lawful for women. The *sa’ibah* is the camel set free due to a vow a man made—that if Allah saved him from illness or he reached his destination, he would do that."

"The *wasilah* is from the sheep. If a ewe gave birth to seven litters—and the seventh was a male—it was slaughtered and eaten by both men and women. If it was a female, it was left among the sheep. If it was a male and a female, they said: 'She has reached her brother,' so it was not slaughtered. Its meat was forbidden to women unless some part of it died—then its eating became lawful for both men and women."

"The *ham* is the stallion that, when its grandson is ridden, they say: 'He has protected his back.' It is also narrated that the *ham* is the camel that, when it has sired ten litters, they say: 'He has protected his back,' so he is not ridden nor barred from fodder or water."

**I say:** There are other narrations from both Shi‘i and Sunni sources regarding the meanings of these names: the *bahirah*, the *sa’ibah*, the *wasilah*, and the *ham*. A portion of them has already been mentioned in the words quoted from al-Tabarsi in *Majma‘ al-Bayan* in the preceding commentary.

What is certain regarding their meanings—as you have seen—is that these categories of livestock were, in the Age of Ignorance, liberated in some manner, having rulings appropriate to that, such as the protection of the back, the prohibition of eating the meat, and not barring them from water and fodder; and that the *wasilah* is from the sheep while the other three are from the camels.

In *al-Majma‘*: Ibn Abbas narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "Amr ibn Luhay ibn Qam‘ah ibn Khandaf had ruled over Mecca, and he was the first to change the religion of Ishmael. He adopted idols, set up images, instituted the *bahirah*, set free the *sa’ibah*, designated the *wasilah*, and protected the *ham*."

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "I certainly saw him in the Fire, the smell of his intestines (قصبه) annoying the people of the Fire." It is also narrated: "dragging his intestines in the Fire."

**I say:** This meaning is narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* through several chains from Ibn Abbas and others.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: ‘Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn Abi Shaybah, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, and Ibn Jarir narrated from Zayd ibn Aslam who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "I certainly know the first who set free the *sa’ibah* animals, set up the images, and the first who changed the religion of Abraham." They asked: "Who is he, O Messenger of Allah?" He said: "Amr ibn Luhay, the brother of Banu Ka‘b. I certainly saw him dragging his intestines in the Fire, the smell of his intestines annoying the people of the Fire."

"And I certainly know the one who slaughtered the *naha’ir* (consecrated camels)." They asked: "Who is he, O Messenger of Allah?" He said: "A man from Banu Mudlij who had two she-camels. He slit their ears and forbade their milk and their backs, saying: 'These two are for Allah.' Then he became in need of them, so he drank their milk and rode their backs."

He said: "I certainly saw him in the Fire, while they were biting him with their mouths and trampling him with their hooves."

In the same source: Ahmad, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi in *Nawadir al-Usul*, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Asma’ wa al-Sifat* narrated from Abu al-Ahwas, from his father, who said: I came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in tattered clothes. He said to me: "Do you have any wealth?" I said: "Yes." He asked: "From what kind of wealth?" I said: "From every kind: from camels, sheep, horses, and slaves." He said: "If Allah has given to you, then let it be seen upon you."

Then he said: "Do your camels give birth with their ears intact?" I said: "Yes, and do camels give birth otherwise?"

He said: "Perhaps you take a blade and cut the ears of a group of them and say: 'These are *bahar*,' and you slit the ears of a group of them and say: 'These are *surum*?'" I said: "Yes." He said: "Do not do so, for everything Allah has given you is lawful for you." Then he recited: **ما جَعَلَ اللهُ مِنْ بَحِيرَةٍ وَلا سائِبَةٍ وَلا وَصِيلَةٍ وَلا حامٍ** (Allah has not prescribed any bahirah, or sa’ibah, or wasilah, or ham).
